The Care Standards Act 2000 says that ‘an establishment is a children’s home if it provides care and accommodation wholly or mainly for children’.

Young people aged 18 and over may also live or stay in a children’s home, but they must be in a minority. See guidance on adults living in children’s homes.

data.info:
The rules that apply in considering whether aerials require planning permission vary depending on what type of aerial they are.

TV or radio aerials do not count as development and so will not need planning permission. Satellite dishes and other types of microwave antenna need planning permission.
data.text: What type of antenna do you want to change or install?
data.policyRef: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, Section 55
data.howMeasured:
TV aerials

TV aerials receive terrestrial television channels like Freeview. They typically have a metal spine and smaller crossbars. The crossbars can be flat or upright, but the aerial always points horizontally.

Radio aerials

Radio aerials receive FM and digital radio signals. They usually look like a large metal circle, a single metal bar, a small cross, or a single vertical rod 30cm to 50cm long.

Microwave antennas (satellite dishes)

Microwave antennas send and receive satellite television or mobile phone data. They have a solid surface like a bowl, plate, dish or drum.

data.howMeasured:
What is a children’s home?
The Care Standards Act 2000 says that ‘an establishment is a children’s home if it provides care and accommodation wholly or mainly for children’.

Children are defined as people who are aged under 18.

Can young people over 18 also live at the property?
Young people aged 18 and over may also live or stay in a children’s home, but they must be in a minority. See guidance on adults living in children’s homes.

What is a change of use?

A change in the way land or buildings are used.

Changes in the way land or buildings are used

The way properties are used puts them into certain categories (or 'classes').

If you want to change how you use a property and that new activity falls under a different category (or use class) you'll need planning permission.


Use classes

The categories given in legislation to different ways of using land and buildings.

data.howMeasured:
Air source heat pumps

An air source heat pump takes heat from the outside air and uses it to warm your home and hot water. It usually looks like a large air conditioning box on the outside of a building.

Ground source heat pumps

A ground source heat pump uses a network of pipes buried underground to absorb heat from the earth. It uses this heat to warm your home and provide hot water.

Water source heat pumps

A water source heat pump takes heat from a local body of water, like a lake, river or stream. It pumps this heat into your home for heating and hot water.

data.howMeasured:
What is a caravan?

Section 13 of the Caravan Act 1968 defines a caravan as:

“any structure designed and adapted for human habitation which can be moved from one place to another”

This definition covers a broad range of moveable structures including:

  • touring caravans,

  • static caravans,

  • motorhomes/campervans

  • pods,

  • shepherd’s huts and

  • mobile homes.

The definition of a caravan does not include tents.  

Twin-unit caravans must:

  • be a maximum size of 20m in length, 6.8m width and 3.05m height

  • made up of two sections maximum joined together, and

  • can still be moved from one place to another when assembled.

What makes a caravan moveable?

Legal cases establish a three-fold test for establishing whether:

  • a structure is moveable and therefore a caravan, or

  • a building

The three tests question these characteristics of the structure:

  • size,

  • permanence, and

  • physical attachment.

data.howMeasured:
Include any shops selling essential goods and covering an area not more than 280m² that are within 1km of the property in a straight line. If you are not sure then you could use a search engine to look for shops in your postcode area as a starting point.

If the property is in an urban area then it is likely that there will be another shop within this distance.

There is currently no standard definition of 'essential'. Other than food it can include hardware and other goods that local homes and businesses may rely on.

data.info:

The rules that apply in considering whether aerials require planning permission vary depending on what type of aerial they are.

TV or radio aerials do not count as development and so will not need planning permission. Satellite dishes and other types of microwave antenna need planning permission.
data.text: What type of antenna will be changed or installed?
data.policyRef: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, Section 55
data.howMeasured:
TV aerials

TV aerials receive terrestrial television channels like Freeview. They typically have a metal spine and smaller crossbars. The crossbars can be flat or upright, but the aerial always points horizontally.

Radio aerials

Radio aerials receive FM and digital radio signals. They usually look like a large metal circle, a single metal bar, a small cross, or a single vertical rod 30cm to 50cm long.

Microwave antennas (satellite dishes)

Microwave antennas send and receive satellite television or mobile phone data. They have a solid surface like a bowl, plate, dish or drum.

data.howMeasured:
What is a use class?

The categories given in legislation to different ways of using land and buildings.

All building uses will fall under:

  • a specific use class or

  • be defined as a 'Sui Generis' use.

There isn't a complete list of use class for every type of building use and how uses are interpreted is subject to case law.

data.howMeasured:
Air source heat pumps

An air source heat pump takes heat from the outside air and uses it to warm your home and hot water. It usually looks like a large air conditioning box on the outside of a building.

Ground source heat pumps

A ground source heat pump uses a network of pipes buried underground to absorb heat from the earth. It uses this heat to warm your home and provide hot water.

Water source heat pumps

A water source heat pump takes heat from a local body of water, like a lake, river or stream. It pumps this heat into your home for heating and hot water.

data.info:
In Greater London, you need planning permission to use your home (or part of it) for short-term stays.

However, you do not need planning permission if both of the following are true:

  • the property is used for short-term accommodation for 90 nights or fewer in a calendar year

  • the person providing the accommodation pays council tax, or is liable to pay council tax, for the property during that time
data.text: Is the property used as a holiday let for more than 90 nights in a calendar year?
data.notes:
JC - is 'pays council tax' and 'liable to pay council tax' the same thing? Service owners asked 14/4/26

AN - simplified content. Added definition of a calendar year.

Previous question: Over a calendar year, is the property used as a holiday let for more than 90 nights?
Previous help text: 
Within Greater London, the use of homes as temporary sleeping accommodation requires planning permission. This applies even if only part of the premises is used as temporary sleeping accommodation.

However planning permission is not required if two tests are met:

(a) it is used for temporary sleeping accommodation for 90 or fewer nights in a calendar year.

(b) the person who provided the sleeping accommodation was liable to pay council tax (which would therefore include people who are liable to council tax but are in receipt of a discount) during the time when the property was used as temporary sleeping accommodation.

When calculating whether it was used for more than 90 nights, you count the number of nights of use as temporary sleeping accommodation, and the number of nights (if any) of each previous use of the premises as temporary sleeping accommodation in the same calendar year.
data.policyRef: Section 25A of the Deregulation Act 2015 Deregulation Act 2015 (legislation.gov.uk)
data.description: A calendar year is 1 January to 31 December.
data.howMeasured: To check if the property was used for more than 90 nights, count all the nights it was used for short-term stays, including any earlier stays in the same year.

data.howMeasured:
Use class E covers commercial, business and services uses:

  • E(a) Display or retail sale of goods, other than hot food

  • E(b) Sale of food and drink for consumption (mostly) on the premises

  • E(c) Provision of:

    • E(c)(i) Financial services,

    • E(c)(ii) Professional services (other than health or medical services), or

    • E(c)(iii) Other appropriate services in a commercial, business or service locality

  • E(d) Indoor sport, recreation or fitness (not involving motorised vehicles or firearms or use as a swimming pool or skating rink,)

  • E(e) Provision of medical or health services (except the use of premises attached to the residence of the consultant or practitioner)

  • E(f) Creche, day nursery or day centre (not including a residential use)

  • E(g) Uses which can be carried out in a residential area without detriment to its amenity:

    • E(g)(i) Offices to carry out any operational or administrative functions,

    • E(g)(ii) Research and development of products or processes

    • E(g)(iii) Industrial processes

data.info:
Retail uses, professional services, restaurants & cafes, business spaces, health centres, gyms and indoor sports facilities (not involving swimming pools, firearms or motorsport) all fall under the same use class (Class E) of the Use Classes Order. Changing the use of a property between these uses does not constitute a 'material change in the use of the property', and therefore does not require planning permission.

Changing the use of a commercial property to anything other than a home, a leisure use or a use within Class E always requires planning permission.

Restaurants or cafes do not include hot food takeaways, as these do not fall under Class E.

If it is considered a building it will need planning permission.

Caravan Sites Act 1968, section 13

Skerritts of Nottingham Ltd v. Secretary of State for Environment, Transport & Regions & Anor ([2000] EWCA Civ 5569)
data.howMeasured:
What is a caravan?

Section 13 of the Caravan Act 1968 defines a caravan as:

“any structure designed and adapted for human habitation which can be moved from one place to another”

This definition covers a broad range of moveable structures including:

  • touring caravans,

  • static caravans,

  • motorhomes/campervans

  • pods,

  • shepherd’s huts and

  • mobile homes.

The definition of a caravan does not include tents.  

Twin-unit caravans must:

  • be a maximum size of 20m in length, 6.8m width and 3.05m height

  • made up of two sections maximum joined together, and

  • can still be moved from one place to another when assembled.

What makes a caravan moveable?

Legal cases establish a three-fold test for establishing whether:

  • a structure is moveable and therefore a caravan, or

  • a building

The three tests question these characteristics of the structure:

  • size,

  • permanence, and

  • physical attachment.

data.howMeasured:
TV aerials

TV aerials receive terrestrial television channels like Freeview. They typically have a metal spine and smaller crossbars. The crossbars can be flat or upright, but the aerial always points horizontally.

Radio aerials

Radio aerials receive FM and digital radio signals. They usually look like a large metal circle, a single metal bar, a small cross, or a single vertical rod 30cm to 50cm long.

Microwave antennas (satellite dishes)

Microwave antennas send and receive satellite television or mobile phone data. They have a solid surface like a bowl, plate, dish or drum.

data.howMeasured:
What is a caravan?

Section 13 of the Caravan Act 1968 defines a caravan as:

“any structure designed and adapted for human habitation which can be moved from one place to another”

This definition covers a broad range of moveable structures including:

  • touring caravans,

  • static caravans,

  • motorhomes/campervans

  • pods,

  • shepherd’s huts and

  • mobile homes.

The definition of a caravan does not include tents.  

Twin-unit caravans must:

  • be a maximum size of 20m in length, 6.8m width and 3.05m height

  • made up of two sections maximum joined together, and

  • can still be moved from one place to another when assembled.

What makes a caravan moveable?

Legal cases establish a three-fold test for establishing whether:

  • a structure is moveable and therefore a caravan, or

  • a building

The three tests question these characteristics of the structure:

  • size,

  • permanence, and

  • physical attachment.

data.howMeasured:
Air source heat pumps

An air source heat pump takes heat from the outside air and uses it to warm your home and hot water. It usually looks like a large air conditioning box on the outside of a building.

Ground source heat pumps

A ground source heat pump uses a network of pipes buried underground to absorb heat from the earth. It uses this heat to warm your home and provide hot water.

Water source heat pumps

A water source heat pump takes heat from a local body of water, like a lake, river or stream. It pumps this heat into your home for heating and hot water.

Use class E covers commercial, business and services uses:

  • E(a) Display or retail sale of goods, other than hot food

  • E(b) Sale of food and drink for consumption (mostly) on the premises

  • E(c) Provision of:

    • E(c)(i) Financial services,

    • E(c)(ii) Professional services (other than health or medical services), or

    • E(c)(iii) Other appropriate services in a commercial, business or service locality

  • E(d) Indoor sport, recreation or fitness (not involving motorised vehicles or firearms or use as a swimming pool or skating rink,)

  • E(e) Provision of medical or health services (except the use of premises attached to the residence of the consultant or practitioner)

  • E(f) Creche, day nursery or day centre (not including a residential use)

  • E(g) Uses which can be carried out in a residential area without detriment to its amenity:

    • E(g)(i) Offices to carry out any operational or administrative functions,

    • E(g)(ii) Research and development of products or processes

    • E(g)(iii) Industrial processes

data.howMeasured:
Air source heat pumps

An air source heat pump takes heat from the outside air and uses it to warm your home and hot water. It usually looks like a large air conditioning box on the outside of a building.

Ground source heat pumps

A ground source heat pump uses a network of pipes buried underground to absorb heat from the earth. It uses this heat to warm your home and provide hot water.

Water source heat pumps

A water source heat pump takes heat from a local body of water, like a lake, river or stream. It pumps this heat into your home for heating and hot water.

data.howMeasured:
A material change of use is when the use of a property, or part of a property, changes in such a way that it changes the use class of the property as defined in the The Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 as amended by the Use Class Order 2020.

For example, if a building previously used as a home is instead used as a shop then this would probably move it from use class C3 (dwellinghouses) to use class E (commercial) and so would be considered a material change of use.

A material change of use is also when the number of planning units on the property is considered to have changed, for example if a second home was built on the same land as an existing one.

data.howMeasured:
Air source heat pumps

An air source heat pump takes heat from the outside air and uses it to warm your home and hot water. It usually looks like a large air conditioning box on the outside of a building.

Ground source heat pumps

A ground source heat pump uses a network of pipes buried underground to absorb heat from the earth. It uses this heat to warm your home and provide hot water.

Water source heat pumps

A water source heat pump takes heat from a local body of water, like a lake, river or stream. It pumps this heat into your home for heating and hot water.

data.howMeasured:
A material change of use is when the use of a property, or part of a property, changes in such a way that it changes the use class of the property as defined in the The Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 as amended by the Use Class Order 2020.

For example, if a building previously used as a home is instead used as a shop then this would probably move it from use class C3 (dwellinghouses) to use class E (commercial) and so would be considered a material change of use.

A material change of use is also when the number of planning units on the property is considered to have changed, for example if a second home was built on the same land as an existing one.

data.policyRef: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, Section 55
data.howMeasured:
TV aerials

TV aerials receive terrestrial television channels like Freeview. They typically have a metal spine and smaller crossbars. The crossbars can be flat or upright, but the aerial always points horizontally.

Radio aerials

Radio aerials receive FM and digital radio signals. They usually look like a large metal circle, a single metal bar, a small cross, or a single vertical rod 30cm to 50cm long.

Microwave antennas (satellite dishes)

Microwave antennas send and receive satellite television or mobile phone data. They have a solid surface like a bowl, plate, dish or drum.

data.howMeasured:
What is the difference between letting and renting?

Both are formal agreements for the use of a property for a specific time period.

Renting includes exclusive use, maintenance and repairs of the property. Often involves longer term lease agreements of six months or a year.

Letting is lease of a property for more flexible time periods and the owner retains responsibility for the maintenance and repairs of the property.

data.howMeasured:
What is a children’s home?
The Care Standards Act 2000 says that ‘an establishment is a children’s home if it provides care and accommodation wholly or mainly for children’.

Children are defined as people who are aged under 18.

Can young people over 18 also live at the property?
Young people aged 18 and over may also live or stay in a children’s home, but they must be in a minority. See guidance on adults living in children’s homes.

What is a change of use?

A change in the way land or buildings are used.

Changes in the way land or buildings are used

The way properties are used puts them into certain categories (or 'classes').

If you want to change how you use a property and that new activity falls under a different category (or use class) you'll need planning permission.


Use classes

The categories given in legislation to different ways of using land and buildings.

Town and Country Planning Act 1990, Section 55(1) https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1990/8/section/55 
Section 55(1) sets out the meaning of development for the purposes of the Act and that includes the making of a material change of use in the use of any buildings or other land. Section 55(2) clarifies a number of operations or uses that “shall not be taken to involve development”. As set out at paragraph (d) that includes the use of any buildings within the curtilage of a dwellinghouse for any purpose incidental to the enjoyment of the dwellinghouse.

data.howMeasured:
What is a caravan?

Section 13 of the Caravan Act 1968 defines a caravan as:

“any structure designed and adapted for human habitation which can be moved from one place to another”

This definition covers a broad range of moveable structures including:

  • touring caravans,

  • static caravans,

  • motorhomes/campervans

  • pods,

  • shepherd’s huts and

  • mobile homes.

The definition of a caravan does not include tents.  

Twin-unit caravans must:

  • be a maximum size of 20m in length, 6.8m width and 3.05m height

  • made up of two sections maximum joined together, and

  • can still be moved from one place to another when assembled.

What makes a caravan moveable?

Legal cases establish a three-fold test for establishing whether:

  • a structure is moveable and therefore a caravan, or

  • a building

The three tests question these characteristics of the structure:

  • size,

  • permanence, and

  • physical attachment.

data.howMeasured:
Air source heat pumps

An air source heat pump takes heat from the outside air and uses it to warm your home and hot water. It usually looks like a large air conditioning box on the outside of a building.

Ground source heat pumps

A ground source heat pump uses a network of pipes buried underground to absorb heat from the earth. It uses this heat to warm your home and provide hot water.

Water source heat pumps

A water source heat pump takes heat from a local body of water, like a lake, river or stream. It pumps this heat into your home for heating and hot water.

The rules that apply in considering whether aerials require planning permission vary depending on what type of aerial they are.

TV or radio aerials do not count as development and so will not need planning permission. Satellite dishes and other types of microwave antenna need planning permission.
data.text: What type of antenna do you want to change or install?
data.policyRef: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, Section 55
data.howMeasured:
TV aerials

TV aerials receive terrestrial television channels like Freeview. They typically have a metal spine and smaller crossbars. The crossbars can be flat or upright, but the aerial always points horizontally.

Radio aerials

Radio aerials receive FM and digital radio signals. They usually look like a large metal circle, a single metal bar, a small cross, or a single vertical rod 30cm to 50cm long.

Microwave antennas (satellite dishes)

Microwave antennas send and receive satellite television or mobile phone data. They have a solid surface like a bowl, plate, dish or drum.

data.policyRef: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, Section 55
data.howMeasured:
A material change of use is when the use of a property, or part of a property, changes in such a way that it changes the use class of the property as defined in the The Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 as amended by the Use Class Order 2020.

For example, if a building previously used as a home is instead used as a shop then this would probably move it from use class C3 (dwellinghouses) to use class E (commercial) and so would be considered a material change of use.

A material change of use is also when the number of planning units on the property is considered to have changed, for example if a second home was built on the same land as an existing one.

data.howMeasured:
What is a self-contained living space?

A few useful tests are that a living space is self contained if:

  • has it's own postal address to the main house

  • physically separated from the main house by fence, wall or hedge

  • the occupier pays the occupier of the main house

  • the occupier doesn't have easy access to the main house

data.howMeasured:
What are related activities?
Activities are 'related' if they support a main use.

For example, a staff canteen supports the main use of a factory. If the factory closed the canteen would no longer be required.

They are 'unrelated' if they function independently, like a car repair workshop and a separate cafe on the same site.

data.policyRef: The Town and Country Planning (General Permitted Development) (England) Order 2015, Schedule 2, Part 14
data.howMeasured:
Air source heat pumps

An air source heat pump takes heat from the outside air and uses it to warm your home and hot water. It usually looks like a large air conditioning box on the outside of a building.

Ground source heat pumps

A ground source heat pump uses a network of pipes buried underground to absorb heat from the earth. It uses this heat to warm your home and provide hot water.

Water source heat pumps

A water source heat pump takes heat from a local body of water, like a lake, river or stream. It pumps this heat into your home for heating and hot water.

data.howMeasured:
What are related activities?
Activities are 'related' if they support a main use.

For example, a staff canteen supports the main use of a factory. If the factory closed the canteen would no longer be required.

They are 'unrelated' if they function independently, like a car repair workshop and a separate cafe on the same site.

data.policyRef:
Caravan Sites Act 1968, section 13

Skerritts of Nottingham Ltd v. Secretary of State for Environment, Transport & Regions & Anor ([2000] EWCA Civ 5569)
data.howMeasured:
What is a caravan?

Section 13 of the Caravan Act 1968 defines a caravan as:

“any structure designed and adapted for human habitation which can be moved from one place to another”

This definition covers a broad range of moveable structures including:

  • touring caravans,

  • static caravans,

  • motorhomes/campervans

  • pods,

  • shepherd’s huts and

  • mobile homes.

The definition of a caravan does not include tents.  

Twin-unit caravans must:

  • be a maximum size of 20m in length, 6.8m width and 3.05m height

  • made up of two sections maximum joined together, and

  • can still be moved from one place to another when assembled.

What makes a caravan moveable?

Legal cases establish a three-fold test for establishing whether:

  • a structure is moveable and therefore a caravan, or

  • a building

The three tests question these characteristics of the structure:

  • size,

  • permanence, and

  • physical attachment.

data.howMeasured:
What is a caravan?

Section 13 of the Caravan Act 1968 defines a caravan as:

“any structure designed and adapted for human habitation which can be moved from one place to another”

This definition covers a broad range of moveable structures including:

  • touring caravans,

  • static caravans,

  • motorhomes/campervans

  • pods,

  • shepherd’s huts and

  • mobile homes.

The definition of a caravan does not include tents.  

Twin-unit caravans must:

  • be a maximum size of 20m in length, 6.8m width and 3.05m height

  • made up of two sections maximum joined together, and

  • can still be moved from one place to another when assembled.

What makes a caravan moveable?

Legal cases establish a three-fold test for establishing whether:

  • a structure is moveable and therefore a caravan, or

  • a building

The three tests question these characteristics of the structure:

  • size,

  • permanence, and

  • physical attachment.

data.howMeasured:
A material change of use is when the use of a property, or part of a property, changes in such a way that it changes the use class of the property as defined in the The Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987 as amended by the Use Class Order 2020.

For example, if a building previously used as a home is instead used as a shop then this would probably move it from use class C3 (dwellinghouses) to use class E (commercial) and so would be considered a material change of use.

A material change of use is also when the number of planning units on the property is considered to have changed, for example if a second home was built on the same land as an existing one.

data.info:
The rules that apply in considering whether aerials require planning permission vary depending on what type of aerial they are.

TV or radio aerials do not count as development and so will not need planning permission. Satellite dishes and other types of microwave antenna need planning permission.
data.text: What type of antenna will be changed or installed?
data.policyRef: Town and Country Planning Act 1990, Section 55
data.howMeasured:
TV aerials

TV aerials receive terrestrial television channels like Freeview. They typically have a metal spine and smaller crossbars. The crossbars can be flat or upright, but the aerial always points horizontally.

Radio aerials

Radio aerials receive FM and digital radio signals. They usually look like a large metal circle, a single metal bar, a small cross, or a single vertical rod 30cm to 50cm long.

Microwave antennas (satellite dishes)

Microwave antennas send and receive satellite television or mobile phone data. They have a solid surface like a bowl, plate, dish or drum.

data.info: The use class of a property describes how it is used in categories. Different use categories allow certain types of changes without planning permission.
data.policyRef: Town and Country Planning (Use Classes) Order 1987
data.howMeasured:
What is a use class?

The categories given in legislation to different ways of using land and buildings.

All building uses will fall under:

  • a specific use class or

  • be defined as a 'Sui Generis' use.

There isn't a complete list of use class for every type of building use and how uses are interpreted is subject to case law.

data.info:
You can make some physical alterations to the property in its current use. When they are complete you can change the use of the property by submitting an application of prior notice.

You need to install the hard surface before changing the use of the property.

Or, if you change the use of a property first using permitted development rights you will need planning permission to alter it.

data.policyRef: The Town and Country Planning (General Permitted Development) (England) Order 2015 Schedule 2, Part 1, Class A
data.description: You'll need planning permission if you change the use of the property before making the physical alterations.
data.howMeasured:
What is prior notice?
A notice to let the Local Plannign Authority know that you want to make certain changes. Within a set timeframe they must let you know if you need to submit priori approval.

What is prior approval?
A mini planning application that is quicker and requires less information that a normal planning application.
